title: neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae
intro: Neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae, commonly known as the "Mexican Devil", is a rare and lesser-known succulent species that belongs to the Euphorbiaceae family. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering health benefits such as reducing inflammation and acting as an antiseptic agent, which can help soothe skin irritations and minor wounds. The therapeutic actions of Neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae include its analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, making it useful for relieving pain and reducing swelling. The bioactive constituents of this plant include triterpenoid saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties, and alkaloids, which contribute to its therapeutic actions.

Additionally, it has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of arthritis and other inflammatory diseases, allowing individuals to maintain their mobility and independence. parts: Neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae has many medicinal parts, such as the roots, stems, and flowers. The roots of Neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae contain medicinal constituents like bufadienolides, which have been studied for their potential in treating cardiovascular conditions. The stems and flowers contain medicinal constituents like saponins and glycosides, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including fever and digestive issues. side_effects: Neolloydia subterranea subsp. zaragosae can have side effects if used improperly, such as respiratory issues, skin irritation, and allergic reactions in some individuals. In rare cases, it may also cause nausea, dizziness, and headaches due to overconsumption or sensitivity to the plant's compounds.
